6badea1f-5c4e-431c-a60f-a233102411cb.title: DNS_CONFULENCE
6badea1f-5c4e-431c-a60f-a233102411cb.summary:
DNS_CONFULENCE SUMMARY

WHAT THIS DOCUMENT COVERS

This runbook provides DNS and Netskope troubleshooting procedures for network engineers and IT support. It addresses issues where users cannot access applications, websites, internal services, or encounter DNS resolution errors including NXDOMAIN, timeouts, and connection failures. The document includes step-by-step diagnostic procedures with special focus on Netskope traffic routing issues that cause DNS to bypass security inspection.

KEY TECHNICAL CONCEPTS

Netskope intercepts DNS queries for domains in its steering policy and resolves them to Netskope gateway IPs in the 191.x.x.x range. This routes traffic through Netskope for security inspection before reaching the destination. If DNS resolves a Netskope-steered domain to any IP outside the 191.x.x.x range (like 103, 52, 142, 104, 34, 13, 8, 40), traffic bypasses Netskope and gets blocked by corporate security policy, making applications appear unreachable even though internet works fine.

DNS translates hostnames to IP addresses by sending queries to configured DNS servers which return the IP address for client connection. NXDOMAIN errors for internal hostnames while external domains resolve indicate internal DNS zone issues such as missing records, zone file syntax errors, BIND9 service problems, or accidentally modified/deleted zone files.

DNS cache issues occur when DNS resolves to old or wrong IP addresses after record changes, requiring cache flushing on both client and server sides. DNS server unreachability causes query timeouts and requires connectivity verification and BIND9 service status checks.

DIAGNOSTIC STEPS AND COMMANDS

Check DNS resolution on Mac/Linux: host <application-url>
Check DNS resolution on Windows: nslookup <application-url>
Test against specific DNS server: nslookup <hostname> <dns-server-ip>
Test against public DNS: nslookup <domain> 8.8.8.8
Check BIND9 status: sudo systemctl status bind9
Check zone status: sudo rndc status and sudo rndc zonestatus <zone-name>
Validate zone file syntax: sudo named-checkzone <zone-name> /etc/bind/zones/<zone-file>
Flush DNS cache Mac: sudo dscacheutil -flushcache and sudo killall -HUP mDNSResponder
Flush DNS cache Windows: ipconfig /flushdns
Flush DNS cache Linux: sudo systemd-resolve --flush-caches
Flush DNS server cache: sudo rndc flush

RESOLUTION APPROACHES

Disconnect and reconnect Netskope client to restore proper routing to 191.x.x.x range. Flush DNS cache on client machines after Netskope reconnection if routing still incorrect. Add missing DNS records to zone files, increment SOA serial numbers, and reload zones with sudo rndc reload <zone-name>. Raise ticket to network team if domain not in Netskope steering policy after reconnection and cache flush. Check application layer issues like VPN requirements, credentials, or application downtime when Netskope routing is correct (191.x.x.x).

WHEN TO USE THIS DOCUMENT

Use this runbook when users report connection errors, ERR_NAME_NOT_RESOLVED, site unreachable, application timeouts, DNS not resolving, NXDOMAIN errors, internal hostname failures, DNS query timeouts, or applications stopped working after DNS configuration changes.

a4b8f465-6a3f-401e-9ad5-ce28fe6251f0.title: check
a4b8f465-6a3f-401e-9ad5-ce28fe6251f0.summary:
SUMMARY: SITE-TO-SITE VPN ARCHITECTURE, IMPLEMENTATION AND OPERATIONS GUIDE

This document provides a comprehensive technical guide for implementing and operating IPsec-based Site-to-Site VPN connections between remote networks. It covers the complete lifecycle from prerequisites and information gathering through packet flow analysis, security mechanisms, and implementation methodology. The guide is designed for network engineers who need to understand not just configuration but the underlying architecture and operational considerations.

KEY TECHNICAL CONCEPTS:

- Site-to-Site VPN establishes secure connectivity between separate networks (branch offices, datacenters, cloud environments) across untrusted networks using IPsec with IKE, ensuring confidentiality, integrity, authentication and secure key exchange between endpoints.

- Packet flow follows a specific sequence: traffic matching at local firewall (e.g., 10.2.0.0/16 to 10.1.0.0/16), IKE negotiation for encryption/hash/DH group exchange, Phase 2 IPsec tunnel creation with inbound/outbound Security Associations, ESP-based payload encryption, Internet transmission, and remote gateway decryption.

- Required firewall protocols include IKE on UDP 500, NAT-T on UDP 4500, ESP on Protocol 50, and AH on Protocol 51. Blocking any of these prevents tunnel establishment.

- Security mechanisms utilize AES-256-GCM for encryption (recommended), SHA-256/SHA-384 for integrity verification, Pre-Shared Key or certificate-based authentication, and Perfect Forward Secrecy to generate new keys per session preventing compromise of historical traffic.

- Prerequisites include static public IPs (or dynamic with DDNS), collected network information from both sites (public IP, internal ranges like 10.1.0.0/16 and 10.2.0.0/16, device types, contacts), NTP synchronization to prevent certificate validation and rekey failures, and proper routing configuration directing traffic toward VPN tunnels.

- Customer information requirements encompass local/remote subnets, VLANs, encryption standards, compliance requirements, bandwidth specifications, redundancy needs, and authentication method preference (PSK for small environments versus certificates for enterprise scalability).

IMPLEMENTATION METHODOLOGY:

The document outlines a 10-step process: collect network information, validate Internet connectivity, verify firewall rules, define VPN parameters, configure peers, perform testing, validate routing, enable monitoring, document configuration, and move to production.

USE THIS DOCUMENT:

Reference this guide when planning new Site-to-Site VPN deployments, troubleshooting tunnel establishment failures, understanding packet flow for security analysis, or creating implementation runbooks for branch office, datacenter, cloud provider, or partner connectivity scenarios.
